name: Neoptolemus
description: Neoptolemus, also known as Pyrrhus, was the son of Achilles and Deidamia in Greek mythology. He was a key figure in the final years of the Trojan War, as the Greeks learned that Troy could not be taken without him and the bow of Heracles (then in the possession of Philoctetes). Odysseus and Phoenix were sent to Skyros to bring Neoptolemus to Troy. He was initially reluctant but was persuaded by the promise of inheriting his father's glory and a share of the war spoils. Upon arriving at Troy, he proved to be a formidable warrior, known for his ruthlessness and brutality. He played a crucial role in the capture of Troy, participating in the massacre and killing King Priam at the altar of Zeus. After the war, Neoptolemus was given Andromache, Hector's widow, as a prize. His life after the Trojan War was filled with further conflicts and tragedies. He eventually met his demise at Delphi, either through an assassination plot orchestrated by Orestes or in a dispute over sacrifices to Apollo. Neoptolemus represents the complex and often brutal realities of heroism in ancient Greek narratives, showcasing the darker aspects of victory and the enduring consequences of war. His story is a powerful exploration of legacy, duty, and the moral ambiguities inherent in achieving glory.
subtitle: Son of Achilles, Conqueror of Troy

keyMyths.title: The Retrieval of Neoptolemus
keyMyths.description: Odysseus and Phoenix travel to Skyros to convince Neoptolemus to join the Trojan War. They promise him glory and a share of the spoils, appealing to his desire to follow in his father's footsteps.

keyMyths.title: The Sack of Troy
keyMyths.description: Neoptolemus participates in the brutal sack of Troy, showing no mercy to the Trojans. He kills King Priam at the altar of Zeus, demonstrating his ruthless nature.

keyMyths.title: Death at Delphi
keyMyths.description: Neoptolemus meets his end at Delphi, either in a dispute with Orestes or due to his own arrogance. The circumstances surrounding his death are debated in various accounts.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: While Neoptolemus doesn't appear directly, the Iliad sets the stage for his importance in the Trojan War's conclusion.
sources.text: While Neoptolemus doesn't appear directly, the Iliad sets the stage for his importance in the Trojan War's conclusion.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: A play focusing on the retrieval of Philoctetes and, implicitly, the necessity of Neoptolemus for Troy's fall.
sources.text: A play focusing on the retrieval of Philoctetes and, implicitly, the necessity of Neoptolemus for Troy's fall.

sources.author: Virgil
sources.description: Provides a vivid depiction of Neoptolemus' brutality during the sack of Troy.
sources.text: Provides a vivid depiction of Neoptolemus' brutality during the sack of Troy.
